title: Ask and Listen
developmental_frame: At 9–10, children can ask questions but often wait to talk rather than truly listen, and their questions tend to be yes/no ones. Learning to ask an open question and actually listen to the answer is a real leap. Interviewing a real person they like — a grandparent, a neighbour, a family friend — is concrete, warm, and gives instant reward. Having learned to *tell* a story in Q1, they now learn to *draw out* someone else's.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is making a real person feel truly listened to, and learning something about them you never knew. At this age that connection — an older person lighting up because a child actually wanted to hear their story — is the whole reward.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child interviews someone they care about and discovers a real story they'd never heard. The warm connection — someone feeling genuinely listened to, and the child feeling closer to them — is powerfully motivating at this age. It turns 'practising questions' into a real relationship moment they'll want to repeat.

artifact: A short interview of a real person: a few good questions asked, answers truly listened to, and one surprising or interesting thing learned, shared back to the family.
success_criteria: The child asked at least two open questions (not just yes/no ones).
They listened well enough to ask a follow-up based on the answer.
They can share one thing they learned that they didn't know before.

lessons.title: Questions that open people up
lessons.concept: Some questions get a one-word answer ('Did you like school?' → 'Yes'). Others open people up ('What was school like when you were my age?'). Open questions start with what, how, or why and invite a real story.
lessons.real_example: 'Was your childhood fun?' gets a shrug. 'What did you do for fun when there were no phones?' gets a whole story. Same idea, very different question.
lessons.apply_task: Write three open questions (starting with what, how, or why) for someone you'd like to interview.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Which of your questions do you think will get the best story? Why that one?
lessons.feedback_task: Read your questions to a parent and ask which would open *them* up most.
lessons.teach_back_task: Help a sibling turn a yes/no question into an open one.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI could suggest generic questions, but the best ones fit the real person you're interviewing — and only you know them. Write your own.
success_criteria: Three open questions are written for a real person.

lessons.title: Really listen
lessons.concept: Listening isn't waiting for your turn to talk — it's actually taking in what the person says. Look at them, don't interrupt, and let there be little quiet gaps. Good listeners make people want to keep talking.
lessons.real_example: You can feel the difference between someone who's really listening and someone who's just nodding while thinking about themselves. Be the first kind.
lessons.apply_task: Practise listening with a family member for two minutes: they talk, you only listen — no interrupting. Then tell them one thing they said.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Was it hard not to jump in? Most people find it surprisingly tricky to just listen.
lessons.feedback_task: Ask the person: 'Did you feel listened to?' Their answer tells you how you did.
lessons.teach_back_task: Play the two-minute listening game with a friend and swap roles.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Listening is a human-to-human skill — a screen can't practise it for you. This one's all about real attention to a real person.
success_criteria: The child listened without interrupting and recalled what was said.

lessons.title: Ask 'tell me more'
lessons.concept: The magic follow-up is simple: 'tell me more' or 'why was that?' It shows you were listening and it unlocks the best part of the story — the bit that doesn't come out in the first answer.
lessons.real_example: Grandpa says 'I had a dog named Rex.' You say 'tell me more about Rex!' — and out comes the whole adventure. The follow-up got the good stuff.
lessons.apply_task: Do your real interview. Ask your open questions, listen, and use 'tell me more' at least twice.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Which 'tell me more' got the best story? Following someone's answer beats sticking to your list.
lessons.feedback_task: Notice how the person responds when you ask for more — do they light up? That's you doing it right.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show someone the 'tell me more' trick and how it unlocks better stories.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Following up means responding to what the *real* person actually just said — you can only do that live, in the moment. No script or app can do it for you.
success_criteria: A real interview happened with at least two follow-up questions used.

lessons.title: Share what you learned
lessons.concept: Finish by sharing the best thing you learned with your family. Passing on someone's story is a kind of gift — and it makes the person you interviewed feel special.
lessons.real_example: 'Did you know Grandpa once had a dog who followed him to school every day?' Now the whole family knows a story that might have been lost. You saved it.
lessons.apply_task: Share the most interesting thing you learned with your family, told as a little story (using your Q1 storytelling skills!).
lessons.reflection_prompt: How did it feel to pass on someone's story? Did the person you interviewed enjoy being talked about?
lessons.feedback_task: Ask your family what surprised them most about what you learned.
lessons.teach_back_task: Encourage a family member to interview someone and share what *they* learn.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Real stories from real people are treasures a computer could never invent. Share this one proudly — it's genuinely yours to tell.
success_criteria: One thing learned was shared as a short story to the family.
The child connected it back to the real person interviewed.
